title: Analytics Dashboard
lede: Your Overview page is mission control for your entire call operation — every call, every minute, every dollar, live on one screen. This guide teaches you to read each number, slice it with filters, and drill from a KPI all the way down to a single call transcript.

steps.title: Open the Overview dashboard
steps.content:
The analytics dashboard is the first thing you see when you log in — no digging required.

  1. In the left sidebar, click Overview (the first item at the top). It is also your landing page after login.
  2. Give it a second to load. From top to bottom you'll see: Total Statistics (all-time counts), four performance cards, a Filters bar, the Calls table, and three charts.
💡 New to the layout itself? The Dashboard Overview guide in Get Started is a tour of where everything lives. This page is about what the numbers mean and how to use them.

images.alt: The Overview analytics dashboard with Total Statistics, KPI cards, filters, and the calls table
images.caption: The Overview dashboard — all-time totals on top, date-filtered performance below

images.alt: The Overview analytics dashboard with Total Statistics, KPI cards, filters, and the calls table
images.caption: The Overview dashboard — all-time totals on top, date-filtered performance below

steps.title: Read Total Statistics — your all-time footprint
steps.content:
The Total Statistics card counts everything you've ever built in iMash. These are all-time totals — they ignore the date filter further down the page.

  • Contacts, Leads, Accounts — the size of your CRM database.
  • Notes and Calendar entries — activity your team and AI agents have logged.
  • AI agents — how many smart agents you've created.
  • Campaigns — outbound calling campaigns, past and present.
  • Phone numbers and SIP providers — your telephony footprint.
  • Knowledge base — files your agents can search during calls.
  • Sub accounts — team member logins under your account.
  • Custom tools — custom actions you've connected to your agents.
  1. To see one team member's slice of these totals, open the All owners dropdown in the card's top-right corner and pick their email.
  2. Clear the selection to go back to whole-account totals.

images.alt: Total Statistics tiles showing contacts, leads, accounts, agents, campaigns, and more
images.caption: Twelve all-time counters — filter them per team member with the All owners dropdown

images.alt: Total Statistics tiles showing contacts, leads, accounts, agents, campaigns, and more
images.caption: Twelve all-time counters — filter them per team member with the All owners dropdown

steps.title: Read the four performance cards
steps.content:
The strip of four cards below Total Statistics is your calling scoreboard for the selected date range. Each card carries a headline number plus a smaller companion metric underneath:

  • Total calls — every call made and received in the window. Underneath: Avg duration, the mean length of a call in minutes.
  • Total minutes — total talk time. Underneath: Success rate, the share of calls that connected and completed rather than failing or going unanswered.
  • Total spent — what the period's calling cost you. Underneath: the effective $ / min rate you actually paid.
  • Avg cost / call — the average price of a single call. Underneath: your average $ / day spend across the window.

All four recalculate instantly every time you change the date range or any filter below — so you can get, say, "total spend on agent Sarah's voicemail calls last week" in two clicks.

⚠️ If the numbers look suspiciously low, check the date range first — the dashboard defaults to Last 30 Days, not all time.

images.alt: Four KPI cards: Total calls, Total minutes, Total spent, and Avg cost per call
images.caption: The performance strip — each headline number has a companion metric beneath it

images.alt: Four KPI cards: Total calls, Total minutes, Total spent, and Avg cost per call
images.caption: The performance strip — each headline number has a companion metric beneath it

steps.title: Pick the time window
steps.content:
Every number below Total Statistics obeys one date range. The Filters card's subtitle always shows the exact dates in play (e.g. Last 30 Days · Jun 10 – Jul 10), so you never have to guess.

  1. In the Filters card, click the date button (it shows the current choice, e.g. Last 30 Days).
  2. Pick a preset: Today, Yesterday, This Week, This Month, or Last 30 Days.
  3. For exact dates, choose Custom Range — a start/end date picker appears right below the button. Select both dates and the whole page refreshes.
💡 A quick habit worth building: compare Today against Yesterday each morning. A sudden drop in Total calls usually surfaces here before anyone notices it anywhere else.

images.alt: Date range menu with Today, Yesterday, This Week, This Month, Last 30 Days, and Custom Range options
images.caption: Six ways to set the window — Custom Range reveals a start/end date picker

images.alt: Date range menu with Today, Yesterday, This Week, This Month, Last 30 Days, and Custom Range options
images.caption: Six ways to set the window — Custom Range reveals a start/end date picker

steps.title: Slice the data with the other filters
steps.content:
Next to the date button sit five more filters. They stack — combine as many as you like, and the KPI cards, table, and charts all narrow together:

  • Phone numbers — only calls on one of your numbers. Perfect for judging whether a new number is pulling its weight.
  • Agent — one AI agent's calls only. Pick Web Dialer to see manual calls placed by your team instead.
  • Tags — filter by what happened on the call. You can select several at once: Calendar (an appointment was booked), Knowledge Search (the agent looked something up), Transfer (call was handed to a human), Agent Ended Call, Customer Ended, No Answer, Voicemail, Silence Timeout, and User Hang up.
  • Owner — only calls belonging to one team member (sub account).
  • Search customer… — type a name to jump straight to a specific contact's calls.

To undo a filter, click the small × that appears inside its box.

💡 Recipe: Tags = Calendar + this week's date range = every appointment your AI booked this week, with the exact calls that produced them listed underneath.

images.alt: Filters bar with date, phone numbers, agent, tags, owner, and customer search controls
images.caption: The Filters bar — the subtitle confirms the exact dates currently applied

images.alt: Filters bar with date, phone numbers, agent, tags, owner, and customer search controls
images.caption: The Filters bar — the subtitle confirms the exact dates currently applied

steps.title: Read the Calls table
steps.content:
The Calls table is the receipt behind every KPI: each row is one call in the current window, newest first. The card header tells you the total and page count. Column by column:

  • Contact / Lead — who was on the call. Click the name to open their full CRM record in a side panel without leaving the dashboard.
  • Agent / Campaign — which AI agent handled it (or Web Dialer for manual calls), with the campaign name underneath when the call came from one.
  • Date & Time — when it happened. The little arrow shows direction: green pointing up-right = outbound, blue pointing down-left = inbound. Your own number that carried the call sits just below.
  • Duration — call length as HH:MM:SS.
  • Type — Phone for regular calls or Web for browser/web-embed calls.
  • Tags — the first pill is how the call ended (e.g. Voicemail, Customer Ended), followed by action tags like Transfer. A +N pill means more — hover it to see the rest.
  • Owner — whose call it is: Main, Own, or a sub user's name.
  • Actions — the play button plays the recording; the eye opens the full call detail (next step).

Use the pagination controls at the bottom-right to move between pages or change how many rows show per page.

images.alt: Calls table with contact, agent, date and time, duration, type, tags, and action columns
images.caption: Every KPI traces back to rows in this table — one row per call, newest first

images.alt: Calls table with contact, agent, date and time, duration, type, tags, and action columns
images.caption: Every KPI traces back to rows in this table — one row per call, newest first

steps.title: Drill into a single call
steps.content:
When a number surprises you, go see the actual call — it's one click away.

  1. In the Actions column, click the eye icon on any row.
  2. The Call Details panel slides open with the recording player on top (or a note if recording wasn't enabled for that call) and five tabs:
  • Call Overview — the call ID, agent name, type & direction, status, duration, and start time at a glance.
  • Transcripts — the full word-for-word conversation between your agent and the caller.
  • Analysis — what the AI extracted from the call (outcomes, captured details).
  • Call Cost — the per-call price breakdown, so you know exactly where each cent went.
  • Observer Events — real-time evaluation events, if you've enabled an observer agent.
  1. Click the × in the top-left corner to return to the dashboard — your filters stay exactly as you left them.

images.alt: Call Details panel with Call Overview, Transcripts, Analysis, Call Cost, and Observer Events tabs
images.caption: From a KPI to the transcript in one click — the Call Details panel

images.alt: Call Details panel with Call Overview, Transcripts, Analysis, Call Cost, and Observer Events tabs
images.caption: From a KPI to the transcript in one click — the Call Details panel

steps.title: Read the three charts at the bottom
steps.content:
Scroll to the bottom for three charts that turn the current view into patterns. They follow your filters, and hovering any bar or point shows the exact value:

  • Reason call ended — a bar per outcome. A tall Voicemail or No Answer bar on an outbound campaign says "try a different calling time"; lots of User Hang up suggests the opening script needs work.
  • Avg duration by agent — mean call length per agent, in minutes. Compare agents doing the same job: an unusually short average can mean callers bail early; an unusually long one can mean the agent rambles.
  • Call volume by hour — calls across the 24 hours of the day. Find your rush hours, then schedule campaigns and staff around them.
💡 The charts read from the calls currently listed in the table, so filter first (one agent, one campaign, one week) and the patterns get much sharper.

images.alt: Three charts: reason call ended, average duration by agent, and call volume by hour
images.caption: Outcome mix, per-agent duration, and hourly volume — hover for exact values

images.alt: Three charts: reason call ended, average duration by agent, and call volume by hour
images.caption: Outcome mix, per-agent duration, and hourly volume — hover for exact values

steps.title: Put it together: three questions you can now answer
steps.content:
The dashboard's real power is combining filters. Try these on your own data:

  • "What did my AI actually book this week?" — Date: This Week → Tags: Calendar. The Total calls card is your booking count; the table lists each booked call, and the eye icon shows the conversation that closed it.
  • "Which agent gives me the cheapest completed call?" — Pick each agent in the Agent filter and compare the Avg cost / call card across them. Pair it with Success rate under Total minutes so you're not just optimizing for cheap failures.
  • "Is my new phone number performing?" — Select it under Phone numbers, set Last 30 Days, and read Total calls plus the Reason call ended chart. Heavy No Answer? Check the hourly chart and shift your calling window.

Once reading this page is second nature, you're running your call center on evidence — not gut feeling.

support.content:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
